is an Australian [public service](/wiki/Public_broadcasting "Public broadcasting") [radio station](/wiki/List_of_radio_stations_in_Australia "List of radio stations in Australia") owned by the [Australian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation") (ABC). or **Double Jay[[a]](#cite_note-originalname-4)** began broadcasting in Sydney on 19 January 1975. It stood apart from [commercial stations](/wiki/Commercial_broadcasting "Commercial broadcasting") and rapidly garnered an audience due to its fringe [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") programming and lack of [advertising](/wiki/Radio_advertisement "Radio advertisement"). From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Australian national radio station

For the boy band formerly known as Triple J, see [Union J](/wiki/Union_J "Union J").

"JJJ" redirects here. For other uses, see [JJJ (disambiguation)](/wiki/JJJ_(disambiguation) "JJJ (disambiguation)").

**Triple J** is an Australian [public service](/wiki/Public_broadcasting "Public broadcasting") [radio station](/wiki/List_of_radio_stations_in_Australia "List of radio stations in Australia") owned by the [Australian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation") (ABC). It aims to appeal to young listeners of [alternative music](/wiki/Alternative_music "Alternative music"), and plays far more [Australian content](/wiki/Music_of_Australia "Music of Australia") than commercial networks.

The station was set up under the [Whitlam government](/wiki/Whitlam_government "Whitlam government") to extend the appeal of the ABC to young Australians. **2JJ** or **Double Jay[[a]](#cite_note-originalname-4)** began broadcasting in Sydney on 19 January 1975. It stood apart from [commercial stations](/wiki/Commercial_broadcasting "Commercial broadcasting") and rapidly garnered an audience due to its fringe [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") programming and lack of [advertising](/wiki/Radio_advertisement "Radio advertisement"). Following a transition to [FM](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") in 1981, the station rebranded to **2JJJ** or Triple J and expanded regionally throughout the 1990s. Two spin-off [digital stations](/wiki/Digital_radio_in_Australia "Digital radio in Australia") were launched in the 2010s: [Double J](/wiki/Double_J_(radio_station) "Double J (radio station)") targets more mature audiences and [Triple J Unearthed](/wiki/Triple_J_Unearthed "Triple J Unearthed") only plays local [unsigned](/wiki/Unsigned_artist "Unsigned artist") musicians.

Despite declining [radio ratings](/wiki/Audience_measurement "Audience measurement"), Triple J remains a dominant tastemaker in the [Australian music scene](/wiki/Australian_music_industry "Australian music industry") and has been historically praised for making [popular culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture") accessible for young people in [regional Australia](/wiki/Regional_Australia "Regional Australia").[[b]](#cite_note-11) Unearthed provides airplay opportunities for [independent artists](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music"), and has helped launch the careers of celebrated Australian musicians. The network also organises its own festivals like [One Night Stand](/wiki/One_Night_Stand_(festival) "One Night Stand (festival)"), and the [Hottest 100](/wiki/Triple_J_Hottest_100 "Triple J Hottest 100"), an annual public poll of popular music. Triple J has long been [criticised](#Criticism) over its [evolving sound](#Music_and_identity) and for promoting a [homogenous music scene](#Homogenisation_of_music).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1970s: Launch and early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1970s: Launch and early years")]

#### Plans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Plans")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Gough_Whitlam_at_the_Lodge_1974.jpg/250px-Gough_Whitlam_at_the_Lodge_1974.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gough_Whitlam_at_the_Lodge_1974.jpg)

[Gough Whitlam](/wiki/Gough_Whitlam "Gough Whitlam") (1974), under whose government Triple J was established

The launch of a new, youth-focused radio station was a product of the progressive media policies of the [Whitlam government](/wiki/Whitlam_government "Whitlam government") of 1972–75.[[10]](#cite_note-JJJHist-12) Prime minister [Gough Whitlam](/wiki/Gough_Whitlam "Gough Whitlam") wanted to set the station up to appeal to the youth vote, and the [Australian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation") (ABC), worried about its declining audience, "wanted a station for young people who would grow up to be ABC listeners."[[11]](#cite_note-Gay-13) A new station was also a recommendation stemming from the McLean Report of 1974, which suggested expanding radio broadcasting onto the [FM band](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting"), issuing a new class of [broadcasting licence](/wiki/Broadcast_license "Broadcast license") which permitted the establishment of [community radio stations](/wiki/Community_radio_stations "Community radio stations"), and the creation of two new stations for the ABC: 2JJ in Sydney, referred to as Double Jay Rock[[a]](#cite_note-originalname-4)[[2]](#cite_note-ABCWhitlam-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Griffiths20250117-3) and the short-lived 3ZZ in Melbourne.[[12]](#cite_note-continuum_6_1-14)[[13]](#cite_note-geocities-15)

Double Jay was intended to be the first link in Whitlam's planned national youth network, however, his administration was not re-elected in the [1975 federal election](/wiki/1975_Australian_federal_election "1975 Australian federal election"). The succeeding [Fraser government](/wiki/Fraser_government "Fraser government")'s budget cuts to the ABC also halted this plan from moving forward.[[14]](#cite_note-Almanac1975-16)[[15]](#cite_note-Fast-17) By the time 2JJ went to air, the [Whitlam government](/wiki/Whitlam_government "Whitlam government") was in its final months of office, and presenters on the station were frequently accused of [left-wing bias](/wiki/Political_bias "Political bias") in the months that followed.[[16]](#cite_note-18)

#### First broadcasts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: First broadcasts")]

Double Jay commenced broadcasting at 11:00 am on Sunday, 19 January 1975, at 1540 [kHz](/wiki/Hertz "Hertz") on the [AM band](/wiki/AM_broadcasting "AM broadcasting").[[12]](#cite_note-continuum_6_1-14) The station was restricted largely to the [Greater Sydney](/wiki/Greater_Sydney "Greater Sydney") region, and its local reception was hampered by inadequate transmitter facilities. However, its frequency was a clear [channel](/wiki/Channel_(broadcasting) "Channel (broadcasting)") nationally, so it was easily heard at night throughout south-eastern Australia. After midnight, 2JJ would use off-air ABC networks to increase its broadcasting range.[[17]](#cite_note-Ricquish040208-19)

| External media | |
| --- | --- |
| Audio | |
| [audio icon](/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_arts.svg) [1975-1985: 40 Years of Triple J](https://abcmedia.akamaized.net/triplej/jfiles_podcast/2015_02_06_40years_1975.mp3?source=web&content_id=10274876), from [ABC Listen](https://www.abc.net.au/listen/programs/the-j-files/1975-1985-40-years-of-triple-j/10274876), Part one of an audio documentary series on Triple J's history. | |
| Video | |
| [video icon](/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_kaboodle.svg) [Double J: 1975](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=G55rb-Gg6vE), [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") video featuring behind-the-scenes content from the 2JJ studio. | |

Its first broadcast demonstrated a determination to distinguish itself from other Australian radio stations. The first on-air presenter, DJ Holger Brockmann, notably used his own name, which, at his previous role at [2SM](/wiki/2SM "2SM"), was considered "too foreign-sounding". After an introductory montage that featured sounds from the countdown and launch of [Apollo 11](/wiki/Apollo_11 "Apollo 11"), Brockmann launched the station's first broadcast with the words, "Wow, and we're away!", and then played [Skyhooks](/wiki/Skyhooks_(band) "Skyhooks (band)")' "[You Just Like Me 'Cos I'm Good in Bed](/wiki/You_Just_Like_Me_%27Cos_I%27m_Good_in_Bed "You Just Like Me 'Cos I'm Good in Bed")".[[18]](#cite_note-Webb-20) The choice of this song to introduce the station was significant, as it represented several important features of the 2JJ brand at the time. Choosing an Australian band reflected the network's commitment to [Australian content](/wiki/Music_of_Australia "Music of Australia") at a time when American acts dominated [pop stations](/wiki/Contemporary_hit_radio "Contemporary hit radio"). Further, the song was one of several tracks from the Skyhooks' album that had been banned on [commercial radio](/wiki/Commercial_broadcasting "Commercial broadcasting") for its explicit sexual content.[[14]](#cite_note-Almanac1975-16)[[19]](#cite_note-21)[[20]](#cite_note-22) The station chose to play songs that were banned from commercial airwaves, including the [Rolling Stones](/wiki/Rolling_Stones "Rolling Stones")' "[Sympathy for the Devil](/wiki/Sympathy_for_the_Devil "Sympathy for the Devil")".[[21]](#cite_note-cresswell2025-23) Because 2JJ was a government-funded station operating under the umbrella of the ABC, it was not bound by commercial censorship codes, and was not answerable to advertisers nor the station owners. In contrast, their Sydney rival, 2SM, was owned by a holding company controlled by the [Catholic Archdiocese of Sydney](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Sydney "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Sydney"), resulting in the ban or editing of numerous songs.[[22]](#cite_note-24)[[23]](#cite_note-25)

The internal politics of 2JJ were considered a radical departure from the formats of commercial stations. 2JJ's presenters had almost total freedom in their on-air delivery, and all staff participated in major policy decisions. For example, as former announcer Gayle Austin reflected: "In early March, women took over the station as announcers to celebrate [International Women's Day](/wiki/International_Women%27s_Day "International Women's Day")", and "The listeners owned the station... and if they wanted to come to the meetings and join the debate, they were welcome".[[11]](#cite_note-Gay-13)

In its early years 2JJ's on-air staff were mainly recruited from either commercial radio or other ABC stations. In another first for the industry, their roster also featured presenters who did not come from a radio background, including singer-songwriters [Bob Hudson](/wiki/Bob_Hudson_(Australian_singer) "Bob Hudson (Australian singer)")[[24]](#cite_note-26) and [John J. Francis](/wiki/John_J._Francis_(musician) "John J. Francis (musician)"),[[25]](#cite_note-27) and actor [Lex Marinos](/wiki/Lex_Marinos "Lex Marinos").[[26]](#cite_note-28) Other notable foundation staff and presenters in January 1975 were Chris Winter, Gayle Austin,[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31) Marius Webb, Ron Moss,[[15]](#cite_note-Fast-17) [Arnold Frolows](/wiki/Arnold_Frolows "Arnold Frolows"), [Mark Colvin](/wiki/Mark_Colvin "Mark Colvin"), [Jim Middleton](/wiki/Jim_Middleton_(journalist) "Jim Middleton (journalist)"), Don Cumming, and [Mac Cocker](/wiki/Mac_Cocker "Mac Cocker").[[15]](#cite_note-Fast-17)[[30]](#cite_note-32) Alan McGirvan was the breakfast announcer. Early staff also included [Ted Robinson](/wiki/Ted_Robinson_(TV_director) "Ted Robinson (TV director)"), [Chris Winter](/wiki/Chris_Winter_(television_presenter) "Chris Winter (television presenter)"), and [Jim Middleton](/wiki/Jim_Middleton_(journalist) "Jim Middleton (journalist)"). Marius Webb and Ron Moss were the station coordinators, while Ros Cheney was programme coordinator, and they established the workplace as kind of [collective](/wiki/Collective "Collective"). Producer and programmer Sammy Collins later said of Cheney that she was "more political and more dedicated than the men", and it was her presence which enabled female representation at every level. Double Jay was the first Australian music radio station to allow women DJs; one of these was Gayle Austin.[[21]](#cite_note-cresswell2025-23)

Before the launch of Double Jay in Sydney, Melbourne was the undisputed capital of music; the new station shone the light on musicians from Sydney musicians, and publicised gigs happening in the city. The station played artists such as [Midnight Oil](/wiki/Midnight_Oil "Midnight Oil"), [Radio Birdman](/wiki/Radio_Birdman "Radio Birdman"), [INXS](/wiki/INXS "INXS"), [Mental As Anything](/wiki/Mental_As_Anything "Mental As Anything"), and [AC/DC](/wiki/AC/DC "AC/DC") long before they had exposure on any other media.[[21]](#cite_note-cresswell2025-23)

#### Rise in popularity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rise in popularity")]

The station rapidly gained popularity, especially with its target youth demographic: media articles noted that in its first two months on air, 2JJ reached a 5.4% share of the total radio audience, with 17% in the 18–24 age group, while the audience share of rival 2SM dropped by 2.3%.[[31]](#cite_note-33) Despite the poor quality of reception caused by the Sydney transmitter, the station still saw rapid growth.[[32]](#cite_note-34) Austin explained that station staff threatened [industrial action](/wiki/Industrial_action "Industrial action") in July 1975 due to the transmitter issues, but officials of the BCB still refused to meet with 2JJ representatives. A new transmitter was not provided until 1980, following the transition to the FM band.[[11]](#cite_note-Gay-13)

After the station hosted an open-air concert in [Liverpool, New South Wales](/wiki/Liverpool,_New_South_Wales "Liverpool, New South Wales"), in May 1975 featuring Skyhooks and [Dragon](/wiki/Dragon_(band) "Dragon (band)"),[[33]](#cite_note-35) city's *[Sun](/wiki/The_Sun-Herald "The Sun-Herald")* newspaper claimed that attendees were "shocked" by "depictions of sexual depravity and shouted obscenities", which allegedly caused women in the audience to clap their hands over their ears, prompting [Coalition](/wiki/Coalition_(Australia) "Coalition (Australia)") frontbencher [Peter Nixon](/wiki/Peter_Nixon "Peter Nixon") to call for the station to be closed down.[[34]](#cite_note-36)

During the 1970s, the music programming varied a lot and depended on the presenters and producers, with various factions favouring different artists and styles.[[21]](#cite_note-cresswell2025-23) There is also archival evidence of [marijuana](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)") being used by presenters while on ABC grounds.

### 1980s: Relaunch and national expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1980s: Relaunch and national expansion")]

On 11 July 1980, 2JJ began broadcasting on the FM band at a frequency of 105.7 MHz (again restricted to within the Greater Sydney region) and became 2JJJ, referred to as Triple J.[[10]](#cite_note-JJJHist-12)[[35]](#cite_note-37) The first song played was another track then banned from commercial radio, "Gay Guys" by [the Dugites](/wiki/The_Dugites "The Dugites").[[36]](#cite_note-Divola051224-38) To celebrate the relaunch, the station organised a concert in [Parramatta Park](/wiki/Parramatta#Parks "Parramatta") on 18 January 1981, featuring [Midnight Oil](/wiki/Midnight_Oil "Midnight Oil") and [Matt Finish](/wiki/Matt_Finish "Matt Finish"), who performed to a crowd of 40,000 people.[[37]](#cite_note-39)[[38]](#cite_note-40)

On 19 January 1981, the AM transmissions ceased, and Triple J became an FM-only station. It was not until the 1989 that the ABC was finally able to expand to [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide"), [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra"), [Darwin](/wiki/Darwin,_Northern_Territory "Darwin, Northern Territory"), [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart"), Melbourne, [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales"), and [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth").[[39]](#cite_note-41) During this period, there were attempts to establish a [playlist](/wiki/Playlist "Playlist") for the whole station.[[21]](#cite_note-cresswell2025-23)

In 1990 the station took [strike action](/wiki/Strike_action "Strike action") after ABC management censored the [N.W.A.](/wiki/N.W.A. "N.W.A.") song "[Fuck tha Police](/wiki/Fuck_tha_Police "Fuck tha Police")". In retaliation, the presenters played N.W.A.'s song "[Express Yourself](/wiki/Express_Yourself_(N.W.A_song) "Express Yourself (N.W.A song)")" 82 times in a row.[[21]](#cite_note-cresswell2025-23) [Triple M](/wiki/Triple_M "Triple M") director Barry Chapman was appointed as general manager to oversee Triple J's network expansion and instil cultural change.[[21]](#cite_note-cresswell2025-23) His tenure generated controversy, most notably in 1990, when all senior announcers in the Sydney office were fired, including the most popular presenters [Tony Biggs](/wiki/Tony_Biggs "Tony Biggs") and Tim Ritchie.[[36]](#cite_note-Divola051224-38) Several protests were held outside its [William Street](/wiki/William_Street,_Sydney "William Street, Sydney") studios, including a 105-hour vigil,[[11]](#cite_note-Gay-13) and a public meeting that packed the [Sydney Town Hall](/wiki/Sydney_Town_Hall "Sydney Town Hall") with angry listeners spilled out onto the street.[[36]](#cite_note-Divola051224-38) Listeners were concerned Chapman would bring a more commercial flair to Triple J with music programming that was less dominated by Sydney acts.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

### 1990s–2000s: Regional and digital expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1990s–2000s: Regional and digital expansion")]

Throughout the 1990s, Triple J commenced expansion to more regional areas of Australia and, in 1994, it was extended to another 18 regional centres throughout the country. In 1996, the total was brought to 44, with the new additions including [Launceston, Tasmania](/wiki/Launceston,_Tasmania "Launceston, Tasmania"); [Albany, Western Australia](/wiki/Albany,_Western_Australia "Albany, Western Australia"); [Bathurst, New South Wales](/wiki/Bathurst,_New_South_Wales "Bathurst, New South Wales") and [Mackay, Queensland](/wiki/Mackay,_Queensland "Mackay, Queensland"). Triple J's most recent expansion was to [Broome, Western Australia](/wiki/Broome,_Western_Australia "Broome, Western Australia") in 2005.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

From the 1990s until around 2010, Triple J "set the cultural agenda, particularly for Australian music". [Grunge music](/wiki/Grunge_music "Grunge music") came to the fore, and bands such as [Spiderbait](/wiki/Spiderbait "Spiderbait"), the [Beasts of Bourbon](/wiki/Beasts_of_Bourbon "Beasts of Bourbon"), and [The Cruel Sea](/wiki/The_Cruel_Sea_(band) "The Cruel Sea (band)") attained critical and popular success, boosted by Triple J's playlist.[[21]](#cite_note-cresswell2025-23)

In May 2003, [Arnold Frolows](/wiki/Arnold_Frolows "Arnold Frolows"), the only remaining member of the original 2JJ staff of 1975, stepped down after 28 years as Triple J music director. He was replaced by presenter [Richard Kingsmill](/wiki/Richard_Kingsmill "Richard Kingsmill"), who joined the station in 1988.[[42]](#cite_note-44)[[43]](#cite_note-Jolly151223-45) Kingsmill had previously worked as a producer and presenter at [2SER](/wiki/2SER "2SER") alongside [Robbie Buck](/wiki/Robbie_Buck "Robbie Buck") and [Tracee Hutchison](/wiki/Tracee_Hutchison "Tracee Hutchison").[[44]](#cite_note-46)

In late 2004, the station's promotion for their annual Beat the Drum contest – in which listeners were to send in the most remarkable places they could promote the Triple J logo – caused brief controversy after it issued a promotional image of the former [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") draped with a huge drum flag.[[45]](#cite_note-47) A notable winner of the competition was a Queensland farmer who formed a drum logo-shaped [crop circle](/wiki/Crop_circle "Crop circle") in his wheat-fields.[[46]](#cite_note-48)

Triple J launched its own music magazine, *JMag* (later known as *[Triple J Magazine](/wiki/Triple_J_Magazine "Triple J Magazine")*), in 2005.[[47]](#cite_note-49) It was initially published quarterly, then monthly, but in 2013 the magazine ceased publication by News Custom Publishing. It returned as an annual edition, produced in-house, until 2016.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

Adapting to the digital [streaming age](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media"), in 2004, the station began to release [podcasts](/wiki/Podcast "Podcast") of some of its talkback shows, including *[Dr. Karl](/wiki/Dr._Karl "Dr. Karl")*, *[This Sporting Life](/wiki/This_Sporting_Life_(radio_program) "This Sporting Life (radio program)")*, and *[Hack](/wiki/Triple_J_Hack "Triple J Hack")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2006, Triple J launched JTV (later rebranded to [Triple J TV](/wiki/Triple_J_TV "Triple J TV")),[[49]](#cite_note-51) a series of television programs broadcast on [ABC1](/wiki/ABC_TV_(Australian_TV_channel) "ABC TV (Australian TV channel)") and [ABC2](/wiki/ABC_Family_(Australian_TV_channel) "ABC Family (Australian TV channel)") including music videos, live concerts, documentaries, and comedy, as well as a behind-the-scenes look at Triple J's studios.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

### 2010s: Double J and Unearthed relaunches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2010s: Double J and Unearthed relaunches")]

In 2006, the website for [Triple J Unearthed](/wiki/Triple_J_Unearthed "Triple J Unearthed") was launched. It remains a hub for [unsigned](/wiki/Unsigned_artist "Unsigned artist") Australian artists to upload their music and be heard by the Triple J team.[[51]](#cite_note-DoubleJ031116-53) A [digital radio](/wiki/Digital_radio_in_Australia "Digital radio in Australia") station, which only plays content from the website, was launched in 2011.[[52]](#cite_note-Murray250811-54)

In 2014, ABC's Dig Music digital radio station was rebranded under the Triple J umbrella, becoming [Double J](/wiki/Double_J_(radio_station) "Double J (radio station)") on 30 April 2014.[[53]](#cite_note-55)[[54]](#cite_note-56) The new station featured both new music and material from Triple J interview and sound archives.[[55]](#cite_note-s3981481-57) Former Triple J announcer [Myf Warhurst](/wiki/Myf_Warhurst "Myf Warhurst"), who hosted the inaugural shift, said "it's for people who love music, and also love a bit of music history".[[55]](#cite_note-s3981481-57) The first song played on Double J was [Nick Cave and the Bad Seeds](/wiki/Nick_Cave_and_the_Bad_Seeds "Nick Cave and the Bad Seeds")' "Get Ready for Love", followed by live performances by Australian artists [Kate Miller-Heidke](/wiki/Kate_Miller-Heidke "Kate Miller-Heidke") and [Paul Dempsey](/wiki/Paul_Dempsey "Paul Dempsey").[[56]](#cite_note-58)

In ratings released in August 2015, Triple J was the highest or equal first in Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, Adelaide, and Perth in the 25–39 demographic.[[57]](#cite_note-59)

Triple J [attracted significant news coverage](/wiki/Triple_J%27s_Hottest_100_of_2017#Background "Triple J's Hottest 100 of 2017") in the lead-up to the [Hottest 100 of 2017](/wiki/Triple_J%27s_Hottest_100_of_2017 "Triple J's Hottest 100 of 2017"), when the station announced they would move the countdown date to the fourth weekend of January, rather than on [Australia Day](/wiki/Australia_Day "Australia Day") (26 January).[[58]](#cite_note-moving1-60) The decision was taken after a listener petition and survey indicated that the majority of listeners would like it changed,[[59]](#cite_note-guiffre2025-61) owing to [sensitivities in the community about celebrating the arrival of the First Fleet at Sydney Cove](/wiki/Australia_Day_debate "Australia Day debate").[[58]](#cite_note-moving1-60)[[60]](#cite_note-moving2-62) The Hottest 100 has successfully broadcast on the fourth weekend of January (but not January 26) since 2018,[[61]](#cite_note-63) with prominent campaigners [A.B. Original](/wiki/A.B._Original "A.B. Original") calling the move "a step in the right direction".[[62]](#cite_note-64)

### 2020s: Kingsmill's departure and restructuring

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2020s: Kingsmill's departure and restructuring")]

Triple J began 2020 with a major overhaul of its hosts, replacing longtime presenters including [Gen Fricker](/wiki/Gen_Fricker "Gen Fricker") and [Tom Tilley](/wiki/Tom_Tilley_(television_presenter) "Tom Tilley (television presenter)") with younger talent including [Bryce Mills](/wiki/Bryce_Mills "Bryce Mills") and [Lucy Smith](/wiki/Lucy_Smith_(radio_presenter) "Lucy Smith (radio presenter)"), in what was billed as a "generational shift for the station".[[63]](#cite_note-65)

|  |
| --- |
| **triple j** (@triplej)  tweeted: |

> did it hurt? when you aged out of the youth radio station

31 August 2021[[64]](#cite_note-Tweettriplej-66)

In August 2021, the Triple J [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") account posted a riff on a popular [pick-up line](/wiki/Pick-up_line "Pick-up line"), which attracted widespread criticism and accusations of [ageism](/wiki/Ageism "Ageism").[[65]](#cite_note-ageism-67)[[66]](#cite_note-68) Writing for *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME"),* journalist Andrew P Street said the controversy renewed discussion about the lack of airplay of older artists on the station, with many being "deemed Double J-ready" in their mid-20s.[[67]](#cite_note-69)

As radio ratings continue to decline across the board due to the rise of [streaming media](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media"),[[68]](#cite_note-70) Triple J saw a 2.5% decline of listeners across the major capital cities between late April and June 2022.[[69]](#cite_note-D'Souza070822-71) Compared to the audience share of 7.7% in the Sydney 18–24 year-old demographic in 2021, the station had dropped to 4.4% in 2022.[[69]](#cite_note-D'Souza070822-71)

In December 2023, it was announced that [Richard Kingsmill](/wiki/Richard_Kingsmill "Richard Kingsmill"), who had been the music director of Triple J and its sister stations, would be leaving after 35 years at the ABC.[[70]](#cite_note-themusic2023-72) During his tenure, he doubled the amount of airtime given to Australian artists, from 30% to 60%, and increased the station's audience from 980,000 in 2006 to 3 million in 2022.[[71]](#cite_note-mediaweek2023-73)[[72]](#cite_note-eltham2023-74) Several news outlets, including *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* and *[Mumbrella](/wiki/Mumbrella "Mumbrella")*, wrote pieces about Kingsmill's importance to the Australian music scene,[[73]](#cite_note-75) with Nathan Jolly of the latter calling the broadcaster "the most important single figure in the history of Australian music", on par with [Michael Gudinski](/wiki/Michael_Gudinski "Michael Gudinski") and [Molly Meldrum](/wiki/Molly_Meldrum "Molly Meldrum").[[43]](#cite_note-Jolly151223-45)

Former head of the [Nova network](/wiki/Nova_Entertainment "Nova Entertainment"), Ben Latimer, was announced as the new head of radio at the ABC amidst a major board restructuring, causing audible "shock and disappointment" in a Sydney staff meeting.[[74]](#cite_note-Green151223-76) Several presenters also announced their departures,[[75]](#cite_note-77) and long-running late night music show *Good Nights* was axed.[[76]](#cite_note-78)

In May 2024, Triple J hosted the inaugural Bars of Steel Live event in [Parramatta](/wiki/Parramatta "Parramatta"), showcasing hip hop and rap artists from all across Western Sydney.[[77]](#cite_note-Connery090524-79) In September, the station also revived its [One Night Stand](/wiki/One_Night_Stand_(festival) "One Night Stand (festival)") regional music festival, which had not run since 2019. It took place in [Warrnambool, Victoria](/wiki/Warrnambool "Warrnambool") to a sold-out crowd.[[78]](#cite_note-Fry240724-80)

In January 2025, the station celebrated 50 years of existence.[[21]](#cite_note-cresswell2025-23)[[59]](#cite_note-guiffre2025-61) To celebrate the event, Double J broadcast the very first day of Triple J's 1975 broadcast, on 19 January 2025 from 11 am to 11 pm, with a two-hour [simulcast](/wiki/Simulcast "Simulcast") on Triple J. Holger Brockmann introduced the replay.[[79]](#cite_note-81) *[Rage](/wiki/Rage_(TV_program) "Rage (TV program)")* also featured a birthday special in January 2025.

A change to higher rotation for new music resulted in a jump in listenership in March 2025.[[80]](#cite_note-82)

## Music and identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Music and identity")]

> Our brief was to provide an alternative to the mainstream, with a heavy emphasis on Australian content. We were to provide opportunities for live and recorded performances by young Australian musicians, and play (shock! horror!) album tracks from all the genres of music that weren't being heard on commercial radio.

Gayle Austin, *Off the Dial*[[11]](#cite_note-Gay-13)

In the station's early years, Triple J primarily played [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") and [pop rock](/wiki/Pop_rock "Pop rock"), but the range of music programmed was far wider than its commercial rivals, encompassing both mainstream and alternative rock and pop, [experimental](/wiki/Experimental_music "Experimental music") and electronic music, [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock"), funk, soul, disco, [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), and the emerging [ambient](/wiki/Ambient_music "Ambient music"), [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") and [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music") genres of the late 1970s.[[81]](#cite_note-Kish010215-83)[[82]](#cite_note-84) Today, the station mostly plays modern rock, [alt-pop](/wiki/Alt-pop "Alt-pop"), [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") and electronic music.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

Triple J initially positioned itself as a "punk" brand due to its fringe and often controversial music programming. The first song played on the station, "You Just Like Me 'Cos I'm Good in Bed" by Skyhooks, was banned from other Australian broadcasters due to its salacious content.[[84]](#cite_note-Griffin200624-86)[[85]](#cite_note-87) The station had also been playing [N.W.A](/wiki/N.W.A "N.W.A")'s protest song "[Fuck tha Police](/wiki/Fuck_tha_Police "Fuck tha Police")" for six months before ABC management caught on, who banned it in 1990. As a result, Triple J staff went on strike and put the group's song "[Express Yourself](/wiki/Express_Yourself_(N.W.A_song) "Express Yourself (N.W.A song)")" on continuous play for 24 hours, playing it roughly 82 times in a row.[[86]](#cite_note-88)[[87]](#cite_note-89)

[![Spacey Jane performing at the Lansdowne Hotel, Sydney, 2025.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Spacey_Jane_performing_at_the_Lansdowne_Hotel%2C_Sydney%2C_2025.jpg/330px-Spacey_Jane_performing_at_the_Lansdowne_Hotel%2C_Sydney%2C_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spacey_Jane_performing_at_the_Lansdowne_Hotel,_Sydney,_2025.jpg)

[Spacey Jane](/wiki/Spacey_Jane "Spacey Jane") were the most played act on Triple J in 2025.

Triple J plays far more [Australian music](/wiki/Music_of_Australia "Music of Australia") than its commercial rivals, and was a pioneer in its coverage of [independent music](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music").[[88]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91) The station has always had a 40% Australian music quota, well above [commercial radio](/wiki/Commercial_broadcasting "Commercial broadcasting")'s 25%—mainstream radio has long been criticised for not playing enough local content.[[69]](#cite_note-D'Souza070822-71)[[90]](#cite_note-92)[[91]](#cite_note-93) Early presenter Gayle Austin reflected in 2006 that before Triple J, "Australian music didn't have much production put into it because there wasn't much money made out of it."[[15]](#cite_note-Fast-17) In 2025, out of the 50 most played artists on Triple J, 70 percent were Australian. The most played act was Fremantle [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") band [Spacey Jane](/wiki/Spacey_Jane "Spacey Jane");[[92]](#cite_note-94) the year prior, it was [indie pop](/wiki/Indie_pop "Indie pop") duo [Lime Cordiale](/wiki/Lime_Cordiale "Lime Cordiale").[[93]](#cite_note-95)

### Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Criticism")]

Triple J is described as "the most discussed, divisive station in Australia's musical landscape".[[7]](#cite_note-:7-8) and for decades has been criticised for sounding too much like commercial radio and losing its initial [punk identity](/wiki/Punk_subculture "Punk subculture").[[84]](#cite_note-Griffin200624-86) According to Sam Whiting of *[The Conversation](/wiki/The_Conversation_(website) "The Conversation (website)"),* it is a "national pastime" to critique the broadcaster for this reason.[[4]](#cite_note-Whiting260624-5) Writers have pointed to increased air time for international pop stars like [Doja Cat](/wiki/Doja_Cat "Doja Cat"), [Olivia Rodrigo](/wiki/Olivia_Rodrigo "Olivia Rodrigo") and [Lil Nas X](/wiki/Lil_Nas_X "Lil Nas X") as proof of this evolution, and that [Billie Eilish](/wiki/Billie_Eilish "Billie Eilish")'s win in the [2019 Hottest 100](/wiki/Triple_J%27s_Hottest_100_of_2019 "Triple J's Hottest 100 of 2019") was an outcome "unthinkable even a few years earlier".[[5]](#cite_note-:5-6)[[69]](#cite_note-D'Souza070822-71) In 2022, the critic Shaad D'Souza said that in recent years the station's programming had been [dominated by garage-pop bands](#Homogenisation_of_music) and overall "consistent to a fault".[[69]](#cite_note-D'Souza070822-71)

### Music data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Music data")]

Launched in 2006 by Paul Stipack, J Play was an online archive of every song played by Triple J over 12 years. It showed an artist's trajectory from their first airing to full rotation. The privately owned site was acquired by [Seventh Street Media](/wiki/Seventh_Street_Media "Seventh Street Media") (Brag Media) along with music publications *[Tone Deaf](/wiki/Tone_Deaf_(magazine) "Tone Deaf (magazine)")* and *[The Brag](/wiki/The_Brag "The Brag")*, in early 2017.[[94]](#cite_note-96)[[95]](#cite_note-97) Owing to changes in the music industry, J Play's usefulness diminished, and it ceased operation in January 2019. The Brag Media retained the J Play database of 40,000 songs, 11,000 artists, and 15,000 [playlists](/wiki/Playlist "Playlist").[[96]](#cite_note-98)

In June 2024, Sydney software engineer Harrison Khannah launched Triple J Watchdog, a similar online resource that tracks each week's top songs, musicians and genres, with additional statistics like artists' country of origin, pronouns and their amount of [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") monthly listeners.[[4]](#cite_note-Whiting260624-5)

## Branding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Branding")]

The Triple J name is stylised in [all lowercase](/wiki/Letter_case "Letter case"), a marketing decision made in 1981[[97]](#cite_note-99) that according to writer [Ben Eltham](/wiki/Ben_Eltham "Ben Eltham") of *[Meanjin](/wiki/Meanjin "Meanjin")*, is one that reflects "a particular world-view that takes brand identity seriously". Marketing staff will vet [press releases](/wiki/Press_release "Press release") and posters to ensure the broadcaster name is written as *triple j*.[[8]](#cite_note-CuriousSignificance-9) In November 2024, the logos of Triple J and its sister stations were updated for the first time in 15 years.[[98]](#cite_note-100) The long-running slogan of Triple J is "We love music",[[99]](#cite_note-101)[[100]](#cite_note-102) though "We love Australian music" has also been used.[[101]](#cite_note-103)

The Triple J news theme introduced in 1991 is a remix of the ABC's "[Majestic Fanfare](/wiki/Majestic_Fanfare "Majestic Fanfare")".

The Triple J News theme is a remix of the [ABC News](/wiki/ABC_News_(Australia) "ABC News (Australia)") theme, "[Majestic Fanfare](/wiki/Majestic_Fanfare "Majestic Fanfare")", originally performed by the Queen's Hall Light Orchestra.[[102]](#cite_note-:3-104)[[103]](#cite_note-:1-105) It was remixed in 1991 by [Paul McKercher](/wiki/Paul_McKercher "Paul McKercher") and John Jacobs, who included a drum sample from [Prince](/wiki/Prince_(musician) "Prince (musician)")'s "[Gett Off](/wiki/Gett_Off "Gett Off")" and the [record scratch](/wiki/Scratching "Scratching") from N.W.A's "Fuck tha Police", a nod to the track after it was banned by ABC management.[[102]](#cite_note-:3-104) This unconventional news theme is another core element of the Triple J [brand identity](/wiki/Brand "Brand")– McKercher wanted to create a sound that was distinctly uncommercial.[[104]](#cite_note-106)

## Programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Programs")]

Main article: [List of Triple J programs](/wiki/List_of_Triple_J_programs "List of Triple J programs")

Triple J's flagship Australian music program is *[Home and Hosed](/wiki/Home_and_Hosed "Home and Hosed")*, broadcast most weekday evenings. It features new local music, artist interviews, and concert news.[[105]](#cite_note-107) On Sunday nights, *[Blak Out](/wiki/Blak_Out "Blak Out")* showcases the latest music from [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_music_of_Australia "Indigenous music of Australia").[[106]](#cite_note-108)

Among the station's live music segments is *[Like a Version](/wiki/Like_a_Version "Like a Version")*, which sees an artist performing an original and a [cover song](/wiki/Cover_version "Cover version"); *[Live at the Wireless](/wiki/Live_at_the_Wireless "Live at the Wireless")*, featuring recordings from concerts and festivals; and *Bars of Steel*, a web series featuring emerging rappers [freestyling](/wiki/Freestyle_rap "Freestyle rap").[[107]](#cite_note-109)

Mark Dodshon[[108]](#cite_note-110) and [Tracee Hutchison](/wiki/Tracee_Hutchison "Tracee Hutchison") were key presenters on *The Australian Music Show* at 2JJJ in the 1980s. The program was a cornerstone of the station's commitment to promoting Australian music, showcasing emerging and established local artists. Dodshon and Hutchison brought their passion and expertise to the show, helping to uncover and celebrate new talent and cementing the program's reputation as a vital space for Australian artists.[[109]](#cite_note-111)[[110]](#cite_note-112)

Through the mid-to-late 1980s, Triple J pioneered special interest programs including the [Japanese pop](/wiki/J-pop "J-pop") show *Nippi Rock Shop*,[[111]](#cite_note-113) [Arnold Frolows](/wiki/Arnold_Frolows "Arnold Frolows")' weekly late-night [ambient music](/wiki/Ambient_music "Ambient music") show *Ambience*,[[112]](#cite_note-114) and Jaslyn Hall's [world music](/wiki/World_music "World music") show,[[113]](#cite_note-115) which was the first of its kind on Australian mainstream radio.[[114]](#cite_note-:4-116) Presenters like Graeme Bartlett and [Tony Barrell](/wiki/Tony_Barrell_(broadcaster) "Tony Barrell (broadcaster)") experimented with the audio format and developed avant-garde programmes like *Sunday Afternoon at the Movies* (1976–77), *Watching the Radio* *With The TV Off* (1978–79) and *Shipbuilding For Pleasure,*[[115]](#cite_note-117) which blended interviews, location sound recordings, music, and found audio to create layered narratives.[[114]](#cite_note-:4-116)

Nowadays, the station continues to broadcast several genre-specific music programs, including:

* *Core*: playing [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music"), [hardcore](/wiki/Hardcore_punk "Hardcore punk"), [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") and [emo](/wiki/Emo "Emo") music
* *[Hip Hop Show](/wiki/Hip_Hop_Show "Hip Hop Show"):* playing [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") music
* *Prism*: playing [alternative pop](/wiki/Alternative_pop "Alternative pop"), [K-pop](/wiki/K-pop "K-pop") and [J-pop](/wiki/J-pop "J-pop") music
* *Soul Ctrl:* playing [neo soul](/wiki/Neo_soul "Neo soul") and [rhythm & blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") music.

News updates on Triple J are written and edited from a youth-oriented perspective. Triple J is the only [ABC radio](/wiki/List_of_ABC_radio_stations "List of ABC radio stations") [station](/wiki/List_of_ABC_radio_stations "List of ABC radio stations") that doesn't carry news bulletins from state or national news ([Radio National](/wiki/Radio_National "Radio National"))—instead, it produces its own with the assistance of ABC resources.[[103]](#cite_note-:1-105) *[Triple J Hack](/wiki/Triple_J_Hack "Triple J Hack")*, the station's flagship [current affairs](/wiki/Current_affairs_(news_format) "Current affairs (news format)") program, is broadcast every weekday evening and features investigations into relevant issues affecting young Australians.[[116]](#cite_note-118)

## Presenters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Presenters")]

Main article: [List of Triple J presenters](/wiki/List_of_Triple_J_presenters "List of Triple J presenters")

Many early Triple J presenters went on to successful careers with commercial stations, the most notable being [Doug Mulray](/wiki/Doug_Mulray "Doug Mulray"), who honed his distinctive comedy-based style at the ABC before moving to rival FM rock station [Triple M](/wiki/Triple_M "Triple M") in the 1980s, where he became the most popular breakfast presenter in Sydney, and one of the highest-paid radio personalities in the country. Presenter [Annette Shun Wah](/wiki/Annette_Shun_Wah "Annette Shun Wah") went on to host the popular *Rock Around the World* series on [SBS](/wiki/SBS_(Australian_TV_channel) "SBS (Australian TV channel)") and is now a program executive with SBS TV and producer of *[The Movie Show](/wiki/The_Movie_Show "The Movie Show")*. Reflecting on the station's 30 year anniversary in 2005, former presenter [Steve Cannane](/wiki/Steve_Cannane "Steve Cannane") said "plenty of musos, comedians, announcers and journos got their start courtesy of the station."[[117]](#cite_note-Cannane070105-119)

From July 2024, presenters on daily programs include:[[118]](#cite_note-120)

* *[Breakfast](/wiki/List_of_Triple_J_programs#Breakfast "List of Triple J programs")*: [Concetta Caristo](/wiki/Concetta_Caristo "Concetta Caristo") and [Luka Muller](/wiki/Luka_Muller "Luka Muller")
* *Mornings:* [Lucy Smith](/wiki/Lucy_Smith_(radio_presenter) "Lucy Smith (radio presenter)")
* *Lunch:* [Dave Woodhead](/wiki/Dave_Woodhead_(comedian) "Dave Woodhead (comedian)")
* *Drive:* Abby Butler and Tyrone Pynor
* *Hack:* Dave Marchese

## Initiatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Initiatives")]

### Triple J Unearthed

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Triple J Unearthed")]

Main article: [Triple J Unearthed](/wiki/Triple_J_Unearthed "Triple J Unearthed")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Missy_Higgins_Photo_by_Joel_Cangy.jpg/250px-Missy_Higgins_Photo_by_Joel_Cangy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Missy_Higgins_Photo_by_Joel_Cangy.jpg)

[Missy Higgins](/wiki/Missy_Higgins "Missy Higgins") says her 2001 Unearthed success led to her initial [record deal](/wiki/Recording_contract "Recording contract") and subsequent success.[[119]](#cite_note-121)

[Unearthed](/wiki/Triple_J_Unearthed "Triple J Unearthed") is Triple J's initiative to support [unsigned](/wiki/Unsigned_artist "Unsigned artist") Australian artists. It began as a [talent competition](/wiki/Talent_show "Talent show") in 1996, which helped discover artists like [Killing Heidi](/wiki/Killing_Heidi "Killing Heidi"), [Missy Higgins](/wiki/Missy_Higgins "Missy Higgins") and [Grinspoon](/wiki/Grinspoon "Grinspoon").[[120]](#cite_note-122)[[121]](#cite_note-123) The Unearthed website was launched in 2006, allowing artists to upload their music to be heard by Triple J staff. It grew to host 30,000 artists and 250,000 users in its first five years.[[122]](#cite_note-124) In 2011, an Unearthed [digital radio station](/wiki/Digital_radio_in_Australia "Digital radio in Australia") was launched, which only plays content from the website.[[52]](#cite_note-Murray250811-54)

Unearthed regularly hosts competitions to improve the recognition of [independent artists](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music"), like [Unearthed High](/wiki/Triple_J_Unearthed#Unearthed_High "Triple J Unearthed"), aimed at musicians in high school, with winners receiving mentoring, recording opportunities and airplay on Triple J. Past winners have included now-celebrated acts like [Hockey Dad](/wiki/Hockey_Dad "Hockey Dad") (2014), [The Kid Laroi](/wiki/The_Kid_Laroi "The Kid Laroi") (2018), [Genesis Owusu](/wiki/Genesis_Owusu "Genesis Owusu") (2015) [Japanese Wallpaper](/wiki/Japanese_Wallpaper "Japanese Wallpaper") (2014) and [Gretta Ray](/wiki/Gretta_Ray "Gretta Ray") (2016).[[123]](#cite_note-RadioInfo140323-125)

### Ausmusic Month

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Ausmusic Month")]

Every November, Triple J celebrates Ausmusic Month, where Australian acts are heavily promoted across all its stations.[[124]](#cite_note-RadioInfo191010-126) A number of events are organised, including major concerts—in 2010 this included headlining acts [Bag Raiders](/wiki/Bag_Raiders "Bag Raiders") and [Ball Park Music](/wiki/Ball_Park_Music "Ball Park Music") and in 2018 featured performances from [Paul Kelly](/wiki/Paul_Kelly_(Australian_musician) "Paul Kelly (Australian musician)"), [Crowded House](/wiki/Crowded_House "Crowded House") and [Missy Higgins](/wiki/Missy_Higgins "Missy Higgins").[[124]](#cite_note-RadioInfo191010-126) Triple J hosts the [J Awards](/wiki/J_Awards "J Awards") during the month,[[125]](#cite_note-127)[[126]](#cite_note-128) and encourages listeners to wear their favourite band's t-shirt on [Ausmusic T-Shirt Day](/wiki/Ausmusic_T-Shirt_Day "Ausmusic T-Shirt Day"), an initiative which the station founded in 2013.[[127]](#cite_note-129)

### J Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: J Awards")]

Main article: [J Awards](/wiki/J_Awards "J Awards")

The [J Awards](/wiki/J_Awards "J Awards") are an annual awards ceremony held in November each year to celebrate [Australian music](/wiki/Australian_music "Australian music"). As of 2024, the five award categories are Australian Album of the Year, Australian Music Video of the Year, Australian Live Act of the Year, Unearthed Artist of the Year and Double J Artist of the Year. The most recent winners for Album of the Year are *[Angel in Realtime](/wiki/Angel_in_Realtime "Angel in Realtime")* by [Gang of Youths](/wiki/Gang_of_Youths "Gang of Youths") in [2022](/wiki/2022_J_Awards "2022 J Awards"), *[Drummer](/wiki/Drummer_(album) "Drummer (album)")* by G Flip in [2023](/wiki/2023_J_Awards "2023 J Awards"), *Zorb* by [Sycco](/wiki/Sycco "Sycco") in [2024](/wiki/2024_J_Awards "2024 J Awards"), and *[I Love My Computer](/wiki/I_Love_My_Computer "I Love My Computer")* by [Ninajirachi](/wiki/Ninajirachi "Ninajirachi") in [2025](/wiki/2025_J_Awards "2025 J Awards").

## Radio events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Radio events")]

* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Olivia_Dean_-_Sabrina_Carpenter_at_BST_Hyde_Park_-_20250706_-_115_%28cropped%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Olivia_Dean_-_Sabrina_Carpenter_at_BST_Hyde_Park_-_20250706_-_115_%28cropped%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Olivia_Dean_-_Sabrina_Carpenter_at_BST_Hyde_Park_-_20250706_-_115_(cropped)_(cropped).jpg)

  British singer [Olivia Dean](/wiki/Olivia_Dean "Olivia Dean") is the latest artist to top the Hottest 100.[[128]](#cite_note-130)

  **[Triple J Hottest 100](/wiki/Triple_J_Hottest_100 "Triple J Hottest 100")**: Broadcast since 1989, the Hottest 100 is an annual poll of the previous year's most popular songs, as voted by listeners. Promoted as the "world's greatest music democracy,"[[129]](#cite_note-131) the countdown has run for over 30 years and attracts millions of votes annually.[[130]](#cite_note-132) From 1998 to 2016, the Hottest 100 was held on [Australia Day](/wiki/Australia_Day "Australia Day") (January 26), but in response to [controversies surrounding the date](/wiki/Australia_Day_debate "Australia Day debate"), the countdown has been held on the fourth weekend of January from 2017 onwards.[[131]](#cite_note-133) The station also occasionally runs speciality Hottest 100 countdowns, such as the [Hottest 100 of the 2010s](/wiki/Triple_J%27s_Hottest_100_of_the_2010s "Triple J's Hottest 100 of the 2010s") in 2020, and the [Triple J Hottest 100 of Australian Songs](/wiki/Triple_J%27s_Hottest_100_of_Australian_Songs "Triple J's Hottest 100 of Australian Songs") in 2025.
* **Requestival**: Broadcast annually from 2020 until 2022, Triple J held Requestival, where they only played listeners' song requests for five days straight.[[132]](#cite_note-134)[[133]](#cite_note-135) Significant airtime was given to songs and artists that would never usually be played on the station, including "[Symphony No. 5](/wiki/Symphony_No._5_(Beethoven) "Symphony No. 5 (Beethoven)")" by [Ludwig van Beethoven](/wiki/Ludwig_van_Beethoven "Ludwig van Beethoven"), "[Shake It Off](/wiki/Shake_It_Off "Shake It Off")" by [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift") (which was notably banned from the [2014 Hottest 100](/wiki/Triple_J_Hottest_100#2015:_#Tay4Hottest100 "Triple J Hottest 100")), "[Rasputin](/wiki/Rasputin_(song) "Rasputin (song)")" by [Boney M.](/wiki/Boney_M. "Boney M."), the theme songs to TV programs *[Antiques Roadshow](/wiki/Antiques_Roadshow "Antiques Roadshow")* and *[Saddle Club](/wiki/The_Saddle_Club "The Saddle Club"),* the entirety of [Julia Gillard's misogyny speech](/wiki/Julia_Gillard%27s_misogyny_speech "Julia Gillard's misogyny speech"), and the [Geelong Cats](/wiki/Geelong_Football_Club "Geelong Football Club") club song, "We Are Geelong".[[134]](#cite_note-136)[[135]](#cite_note-137) In 2021, more than 80,000 song requests were submitted by listeners.[[136]](#cite_note-138) The event was similar in concept to *[Super Request](/wiki/Super_Request "Super Request"),* a nightly music request show that aired from 1998 to 2001.
* **[Impossible Music Festival](/wiki/Impossible_Music_Festival "Impossible Music Festival")**: Broadcast annually from 2005 to 2008, the Impossible Music Festival consisted of 55 live music recordings played consecutively over one weekend. The lineup of artists each time was decided by listeners, and recordings were derived from festivals, concerts, pub gigs and studio sessions.

## Live events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Live events")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Triple_J_One_Night_Stand_map_2025.jpg/330px-Triple_J_One_Night_Stand_map_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Triple_J_One_Night_Stand_map_2025.jpg)

Map of the towns where [One Night Stand](/wiki/One_Night_Stand_(festival) "One Night Stand (festival)") has been hosted, as of 2025.

Triple J routinely sponsors [live music](/wiki/Concert "Concert") events in Australia,[[137]](#cite_note-:6-139) and has organised its own festivals and concerts, including:

* **[One Night Stand](/wiki/One_Night_Stand_(festival) "One Night Stand (festival)")**: Hosted almost every year since 2004, One Night Stand is an all-ages concert series held in different regional towns.[[138]](#cite_note-abcfarewell-140) It is up to the audience to nominate their regional towns to host the event, needing to provide examples of local support, including community (signatures), local government (council approval), and a venue for the concert.[[139]](#cite_note-141)
* **Bars of Steel Live**: Based on their [web series](/wiki/Web_series "Web series"), Triple J hosted a free concert in [Parramatta](/wiki/Parramatta "Parramatta") in May 2024, featuring hip hop and rap artists from all across [Western Sydney](/wiki/Greater_Western_Sydney "Greater Western Sydney").[[77]](#cite_note-Connery090524-79) The inaugural lineup included [Youngn Lipz](/wiki/Youngn_Lipz "Youngn Lipz"), [A.Girl](/wiki/A.Girl "A.Girl"), [Becca Hatch](/wiki/Becca_Hatch "Becca Hatch") and Unearthed competition winners. Triple J also hosted several songwriting and music production workshops.[[140]](#cite_note-142)
* **[Beat the Drum](/wiki/Beat_the_Drum_(album) "Beat the Drum (album)")**: On 16 January 2015, to commemorate the 40th anniversary of Triple J, a one-off concert called Beat the Drum was held at [the Domain, Sydney](/wiki/The_Domain,_Sydney "The Domain, Sydney"). Hosted by [Peter Garrett](/wiki/Peter_Garrett "Peter Garrett"), the list of performers, all of whom are the beneficiaries of the station's support, included [Hilltop Hoods](/wiki/Hilltop_Hoods "Hilltop Hoods"), [the Presets](/wiki/The_Presets "The Presets"), [the Cat Empire](/wiki/The_Cat_Empire "The Cat Empire"), [You Am I](/wiki/You_Am_I "You Am I"), [Daniel Johns](/wiki/Daniel_Johns "Daniel Johns"), [Joelistics](/wiki/Joelistics "Joelistics"), [Ball Park Music](/wiki/Ball_Park_Music "Ball Park Music"), [Adalita](/wiki/Adalita_Srsen "Adalita Srsen"), [Vance Joy](/wiki/Vance_Joy "Vance Joy"), and [Gotye](/wiki/Gotye "Gotye"). A recording of the event was released on CD and DVD in May 2015, which won the [ARIA Award for Best Original Soundtrack, Cast or Show Album](/wiki/ARIA_Award_for_Best_Original_Soundtrack,_Cast_or_Show_Album "ARIA Award for Best Original Soundtrack, Cast or Show Album").
* **Tribute concerts**: In November 2009, Triple J hosted a [tribute concert series](/wiki/Tribute_act "Tribute act") for [Paul Kelly](/wiki/Paul_Kelly_(Australian_musician) "Paul Kelly (Australian musician)") called [Before Too Long](/wiki/Before_Too_Long:_Triple_J%27s_Tribute_to_Paul_Kelly "Before Too Long: Triple J's Tribute to Paul Kelly"), which ran over two nights at Melbourne's [Forum Theatre](/wiki/Forum_Theatre "Forum Theatre").[[138]](#cite_note-abcfarewell-140)[[141]](#cite_note-143) In 2011, another live set of shows was presented, this time honouring [Nick Cave](/wiki/Nick_Cave "Nick Cave"), in a show called [Straight to You](/wiki/Straight_to_You_%E2%80%93_Triple_J%27s_Tribute_to_Nick_Cave "Straight to You – Triple J's Tribute to Nick Cave"). Both events were recorded to produce two commercially successful live albums.[[142]](#cite_note-144)

## Impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Impact")]

### Artist discovery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Artist discovery")]

Triple J has substantial influence over the Australian music market and is a tastemaker.[[4]](#cite_note-Whiting260624-5) Since its inception, emerging artists it has championed have become some of the country's most celebrated musical acts, including [Midnight Oil](/wiki/Midnight_Oil "Midnight Oil"), [Nick Cave](/wiki/Nick_Cave "Nick Cave"), [Silverchair](/wiki/Silverchair "Silverchair") and the [John Butler Trio](/wiki/John_Butler_Trio "John Butler Trio").[[117]](#cite_note-Cannane070105-119) The station is also credited for creating local audiences for international acts, like [Blondie](/wiki/Blondie_(band) "Blondie (band)"), [Devo](/wiki/Devo "Devo"), [Garbage](/wiki/Garbage_(band) "Garbage (band)") and [the B-52s](/wiki/The_B-52s "The B-52s")—Triple J was the first station in the world to play the latter's debut single "[Rock Lobster](/wiki/Rock_Lobster "Rock Lobster")".[[6]](#cite_note-:0-7)

More recently, artists platformed via the network's discovery channel, [Triple J Unearthed](/wiki/Triple_J_Unearthed "Triple J Unearthed"), have included [Flume](/wiki/Flume_(musician) "Flume (musician)"), [Missy Higgins](/wiki/Missy_Higgins "Missy Higgins"), [Vance Joy](/wiki/Vance_Joy "Vance Joy"), [Gang of Youths](/wiki/Gang_of_Youths "Gang of Youths"), [Grinspoon](/wiki/Grinspoon "Grinspoon") and [the Kid Laroi](/wiki/The_Kid_Laroi "The Kid Laroi"). Columnist Peter Vincent has written Triple J is "the envy of the music scenes across the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), for its ability to deliver new local talent to mass audiences, quickly."[[5]](#cite_note-:5-6)

The station has faced accusations of having a [monopoly](/wiki/Monopoly "Monopoly") on new music, with emerging acts heavily relying on their support for career growth.[[5]](#cite_note-:5-6) Notable musicians including [Matty Healy](/wiki/Matty_Healy "Matty Healy") of [the 1975](/wiki/The_1975 "The 1975") and [Andrew Stockdale](/wiki/Andrew_Stockdale "Andrew Stockdale") of [Wolfmother](/wiki/Wolfmother "Wolfmother") have both publicly criticised Triple J, despite enjoying extensive support from the station.[[143]](#cite_note-145)[[144]](#cite_note-146) Perth rock outfit [End of Fashion](/wiki/End_of_Fashion "End of Fashion") claimed that reduced airplay ended the band's career.[[145]](#cite_note-147)

In the mid-2020s, there has been renewed debate about the role of Triple J in a music industry dominated by [streaming services](/wiki/Music_streaming_service "Music streaming service").[[146]](#cite_note-148)

### Homogenisation of music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Homogenisation of music")]

Triple J has routinely been accused of promoting a homogeneous Australian music scene. Concerns were aired in [Fairfax](/wiki/Fairfax_Media "Fairfax Media") newspapers in 2014 with notable musicians, remaining anonymous, claiming artists needed the "Triple J sound" to be played on the station.[[147]](#cite_note-149)[[148]](#cite_note-Newstead1201142-150) Julie Fenwick of *[Vice](/wiki/Vice_(magazine) "Vice (magazine)")* defined this sound as "a sort of indie surf, slightly pop persuasion."[[149]](#cite_note-151) Musician [Whitley](/wiki/Whitley_(singer) "Whitley (singer)") believed the broadcaster had failed to "challenge and present new ideas for the youth of Australia."[[150]](#cite_note-152) Music director Dave Ruby Howe acknowledged there were some similar sounds on discovery platform Unearthed, but bands purely chasing airplay would get caught out.[[148]](#cite_note-Newstead1201142-150)

### Music business

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Music business")]

> On any given day, hundreds of thousands of listeners across the country are tuned in. Label owners, promoters, publicists and musicians follow the station with relentless fascination, as its playlist and musical preferences can literally make, delay, or break careers in the notoriously fickle music business.

[Andrew McMillen](/wiki/Andrew_McMillen "Andrew McMillen"), *The Discovery Channel*, 6 January 2012[[151]](#cite_note-DiscoveryChannel-153)

Triple J has sponsored Australian live music events since its inception, and has organised [its own festivals](#Live_events) like Bars of Steel Live and [One Night Stand](/wiki/One_Night_Stand_(festival) "One Night Stand (festival)"), the latter of which has run in small regional towns since 2004.[[84]](#cite_note-Griffin200624-86) In 2024, Triple J and Double J supported over 60 festivals and tours of international and domestic artists. They promote such events in their radio programs and commercials and on their social media channels.[[137]](#cite_note-:6-139)

In its early years, Triple J also played an important role in record distribution. [Labels](/wiki/Record_label "Record label") would previously only import recordings that they knew would yield good commercial return, leaving them often unwilling to take risks on local releases. For example, Australian distributors initially refused to offer [801](/wiki/801_(band) "801 (band)")'s live album *[801 Live](/wiki/801_Live "801 Live")* in the country, but constant airplay on Triple J made the record the highest selling import album of the year. Thus, the label decided to release it locally.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Alternative radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_J&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Alternative radio")]

> As cringe-worthy as Triple J can sometimes be, the network is irreplaceable; it fills a unique niche in the Australian cultural landscape.

[Ben Eltham](/wiki/Ben_Eltham "Ben Eltham"), *The Curious Significance of Triple J*[[8]](#cite_note-CuriousSignificance-9)

Triple J's programming approach was copied by succeeding commercial stations. [Nova](/wiki/Nova_(radio_network) "Nova (radio network)"), conceived as a competitor youth station, had a "clearly borrowed" catalogue from Triple J, but was slightly more conservative with its song selections.[[152]](#cite_note-154)

Former [Australian Recording Industry Association](/wiki/Australian_Recording_Industry_Association "Australian Recording Industry Association") staffer Danny Yau said that Triple J's nationalisation from the early 1990s created a new role for local [community radio](/wiki/Community_radio "Community radio") stations, particularly Sydney's [FBi](/wiki/FBi_Radio "FBi Radio") and Melbourne's [3RRR](/wiki/3RRR "3RRR"), to fill the broadcaster's gaps with more niche regional content.[[153]](#cite_note-155)
